title: Conclusion: What We Now Know
content:
Recent experience shows that local government reorganisation can deliver benefits, but only where complexity is acknowledged and actively managed. Workforce disruption is often greater, transition costs higher, and service impacts more pronounced than business cases typically suggest.

These outcomes are not inevitable. They reflect choices about realism, sequencing, resourcing and governance. Councils that have navigated reorganisation most effectively are those that invested early in programme capacity, protected frontline delivery, and treated people, services and democratic structures as central to success.

The lesson for future reorganisations is clear. Structural change must be designed around delivery reality, not organisational diagrams. Where that happens, reform can strengthen local government. Where it does not, the risks are now well understood, and should no longer come as a surprise.
